content.subtitle: Overview
items: Past simple and past continuous forms can describe unreal or hypothetical meaning in the present, future or in general statements.
Past perfect simple and past perfect continuous can describe unreal situations in the past that did not actually happen.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
content.description: With I/he/she/it after these unreal structures, both was and were may appear.

content.subtitle: Note
content.description: In formal written English, were is often preferred, and it is the fixed choice in the phrase If I were you ...

content.subtitle: Use
items: For unlikely or imaginary present/future/general conditions: I wouldn't sign the contract unless I **was** absolutely sure of the terms.
For unreal past conditions and results: If I **had checked** the timetable, I would have arrived earlier.

content.subtitle: Use
items: To discuss unlikely present, future or general situations: Supposing you **were offered** a year abroad, would you accept?
To discuss unreal past situations: Imagine you **had taken** that role last year. How different would things be now?

content.subtitle: Watch out!
content.description: A present tense after these words often suggests a more realistic possibility; a past form suggests lower probability.
examples: Supposing you **become** self-employed, how will your routine change? (more likely)
Supposing you **became** self-employed, how would your routine change? (less likely)

content.subtitle: Use
items: For unreal comparisons in the present/future/in general: He talks as if he **were** in charge of the whole project.
For unreal comparisons about the past: They reacted as though they **had never heard** the news before.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: After a present or present perfect main verb, a past form after as if/as though usually marks the comparison as unreal.
If the comparison is considered possible, a present or present perfect form can follow as if/as though.
After a past main verb, past forms after as if/as though are normal whether the comparison is real or unreal.

content.subtitle: Use
items: To make questions and requests sound softer and more polite: I was wondering if you could send me the updated file.

content.subtitle: Use
items: To say something should happen now or very soon: It's about time we **made** a final decision.

content.subtitle: Note
content.description: After it's time, both past simple and past continuous can be used with very similar meaning.
items: A full infinitive can follow it's time, but not the stronger forms it's high time / it's about time.

content.subtitle: Use
items: To express preference about another person's present/future/general action: I'd rather you **didn't mention** this yet.
To express preference about another person's past action: We'd sooner she **had told** us earlier.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
content.description: Use a past form after would rather/sooner only when the subject changes; with the same subject, use an infinitive structure.
examples: ✓ I'd prefer **to stay** at home tonight.

content.subtitle: Use
items: Wishes about now, the future or in general: I wish I **had** more free time during the week.
Wishes about the past: If only we **had left** earlier, we wouldn't have missed the train.

content.subtitle: Other structures with wish / if only
items: Wish / If only + would → criticism or a desire for someone/something to change
Wish / If only + could → wish about ability or permission
Wish / If only + could + perfect infinitive → wish about past ability/permission
Wish + full infinitive → very formal expression of intention
Wish + noun → fixed expressions of goodwill

content.subtitle: Important Notes
items: Would is normally avoided when the subject of wish and would is the same person.
Use hope (not wish) for expected or likely future outcomes.

sections.title: A: The Paper Trail
sections.instructions: Write the verbs in brackets in the correct form in each gap.

items.sentence: There is an intriguing historical puzzle that asks: 'If the ancient Chinese (1) ...... (have) all the information-sharing technology available to us today, would the exchange of ideas across the ancient world have happened more rapidly?'
(2) ...... (we / be) to consider this logically, we would find it nearly impossible to arrive at a satisfying conclusion.
Nevertheless, approaching it with creativity reveals a striking insight. (3) ...... (the ancient Chinese / be) as connected as the modern world, they would still have faced one remarkable bottleneck.

What bottleneck? Consider that if, over two thousand years ago, they (4) ...... (employ) a scribe to distribute royal decrees across the empire in a single day, he would have needed a far lighter writing material than clay or stone tablets.

Many ancient civilisations had no equivalent of paper, so there would have been no efficient means of mass written communication. Of course, the truth is that if these hypothetical societies (5) ...... (have) access to paper, it would be impossible for them to have had it without first mastering the art of pulp-making.
If the technique of paper-making (6) ...... (develop), we would not have the publishing industry, the education system, or the written exam as we know it.

Modern education depends absolutely on the widespread availability of affordable written materials. It may seem obvious to us now that paper should exist, but early philosophers found its very concept troubling. Medieval scholars debated endlessly: 'If nothing solid (7) ...... (be) reducible to fibres, then paper itself would be a kind of impossibility.'
'And if it (8) ...... (do) turn out to be possible, then the entire notion of impermanence in writing would be overturned.'
If it (9) ...... (be) for a Chinese court official named Cai Lun, who refined the papermaking process around 105 AD, who knows how differently civilisation might have evolved?

He produced the earliest recorded account of true papermaking using bark, hemp, and rags. If he (10) ...... (do), the book — and everything that followed from it — might never have come into existence.

sections.title: B: Hypothetical Situations
sections.instructions: Choose the correct option (A, B or C) for each sentence.

items.sentence: Supposing you **had / have / having** the opportunity to spend a year studying at a university abroad, would you take it?
What if we **decided / decide / will decide** to give up our jobs and travel the world for a year?
Suppose all your colleagues **said / say / had said** they found you difficult to work with and you found out. How would you respond?
Imagine you **didn't have / don't have / hadn't had** access to a phone, computer, or any digital device for an entire month. How would you manage?
What if the printing press **hadn't been invented / wasn't invented / weren't invented**? How might history have unfolded differently?
Supposing all public transport **stopped / stops / had stopped** operating overnight, how would people get to work the next morning?
Imagine we **had just been told / were just told / have just been told** that a severe storm was heading directly towards the city. What steps would be most important to take?
Suppose you **could / can / will be able to** speak any language in the world fluently without studying it. Which would you choose?
What if every country in the world **spoke / speaks / had spoken** a different invented language with no shared tongue?
Suppose it **were proved / is proved / has been proved** that certain plants were capable of experiencing pain.
I know it's almost certainly not going to happen, but imagine this small café **became / becomes / had become** the most celebrated restaurant in the country!
What if you **were given / are given / had been given** a private island to develop entirely as you wished? What would you build first?
Supposing you **woke / wake / had woken** up one morning to find yourself in a completely unfamiliar country, how would you go about finding your way?
I know it's physically impossible, but suppose for a moment that water **were / is / had been** able to flow naturally upward.
Imagine that humans **couldn't / can't / hadn't been able to** use written language. How do you think civilisation would have developed?

sections.title: C: As if / As though (Comprehension)
sections.instructions: Look at the questions that follow each sentence and circle the answer, A, B or C which is not correct.

items.sentence: Why does Elena always behave as if she were the only competent person in the whole department?
Patrick often talks as if he had grown up in an extremely privileged household.
The hikers look as if they've been walking uphill for hours.
Don't worry about bringing it up. Just speak to her as if you didn't know she'd been through such a difficult period.
It was really unsettling. At one point, it felt like we weren't ever going to find our way back through the fog.
Omar looked as if he had just received some unexpectedly wonderful news.

sections.title: D: As if / As though (Verb Forms)
sections.instructions: Write the verbs in brackets in the correct form in each gap. When a situation is hypothetical or unlikely, use unreal tenses.

items.sentence: Nadia speaks as though she ……………………. (grow up) in Paris, yet she only moved there six months ago.
By the time Carlos arrived at the venue, he looked as though he ………………………… (drag) through a muddy field.
The pain in my knee was so severe that I honestly felt as if I ………………………… (go) to collapse right there on the pitch.
When I saw Priya at the conference last Thursday, it felt as if she ………………………… (give) keynote speeches for years. I couldn't believe it was actually her first one!
Theo looked as if he ………………………… (receive) the worst possible news. In fact, he'd just been told the project was cancelled.
Don't you think Sophie sometimes looks as though she ………………………… (wish) she had pursued a completely different career path?
Marcus always dresses as though he ………………………… (roll) in money, yet everyone knows he's barely making ends meet.
You two are acting as if tomorrow ………………………… (be) a public holiday. I assure you, it isn't!
How can you just sit there so calmly as if nothing ………………………… (happen)?

sections.title: E: Polite Questions and Requests
sections.instructions: Make each of these statements or questions as polite as possible by using past tenses.

items.sentence: What size ...... (you, want) to try on, sir?
I ...... (look) for a suitable anniversary present for my parents.
We ...... (wonder) whether you ...... (want) to join us for dinner on Friday evening.
Excuse me. ...... (you, plan) to leave your luggage here for long?
Could I ask if ...... (you, wish) to make a reservation, or just have a look at the menu?
May I ask whether ...... (you, look) for something in a specific price range?
...... (you, want) still or sparkling water with your meal?
I ...... (hope) you ...... (give) us a little more time to think it over.
How long ...... (you, intend) to keep the vehicle?
I ...... (wonder) if you ...... (pay) by card or in cash, madam.

sections.title: F: It's (high/about) time
sections.instructions: Write a verb from the box in the correct form in each gap.

items.sentence: It's high time you ………………… complaining and did something constructive about it.
It's time you ………………… into renting your own flat. You're nearly thirty!
I really think it's time we ………………… a new washing machine. This one has been breaking down constantly.
It's time for the committee ………………… something about the car park situation.
It's about time the local council ………………… investing in the park again.
Come on, Leo. It's time ………………… or you'll miss your train.
Don't you think it's time you ………………… a promotion after all these years?
It's time for her ………………… team leader — she's more than earned it.
It's definitely time I ………………… to make my own decisions around here.
We really should make a move. It's time for us ………………… before the traffic gets bad.
Isn't it about time he ………………… to manage his own finances properly?
Is this a good moment ………………… the clinic, or would it be better to wait until this afternoon?

sections.title: G: Would rather / sooner
sections.instructions: Write a word or short phrase in each gap so that each second sentence has a similar meaning to the first sentence.

items.sentence: The director would prefer the new intern to have some prior experience in the field. The director would rather the new intern ……………………………………… some experience in the field.
Would you prefer me to return after lunch? Would you rather …………………………………… after lunch?
Quite frankly, I'd prefer you not to mention this to anyone else. Quite frankly, I'd rather …………………………………… anyone else about this.
I'd prefer us to get a new boiler rather than go on a costly city break this winter. I'd sooner ………………………………………… a new boiler than ………………………………… on a costly city break this winter.
Mum said she'd prefer Lucas to be living closer to home, and I agree entirely. Mum said she'd rather Lucas …………………………………… closer to home, and I agree entirely.
I would prefer her to be enrolled on a training programme rather than made redundant. I would rather ………………………………………… on a training programme than made redundant.
Honestly, I'd prefer us not to bring this up in front of the children, if that's alright. Honestly, I'd sooner ………………………………………… about this in front of the children, if that's alright.
Of course I would have preferred you to be upfront with me from the very beginning! Of course I would rather ………………………………… me the truth from the beginning!
I believe Elisa would definitely prefer to have a small, intimate ceremony. I believe Elisa would definitely rather ………………………………… a small, intimate ceremony.
We would have preferred them not to have overstayed their welcome, if we're being honest. We would sooner …………………………………… their welcome, if we're being honest.

sections.title: H: Wish / If only
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase. If both are correct, circle both.

items.sentence: All my classmates have laptops. I wish I **have / had** one too!
Don't you sometimes wish you **ran / were running** your own business?
If only we **didn't leave / hadn't left** our umbrellas at home!
If only we **don't / didn't** have to work this weekend!
I do wish the plumber **came / would come**. The leak is getting worse.
Do you ever wish that you **sang / could sing** opera really well?
There is one thing I truly regret. I wish I could **go / have gone** to see that concert, but at the time it just wasn't possible.
Excuse me, I wish **spoke / to speak** to your manager, please.
I **wish / hope** the weather holds for the weekend!
Why on earth does Marcus wish he **would be promoted / they would give him** a raise already?
It's probably not the wisest investment, but I do wish you **luck / lucky** with it.
My grandmother always wished that she was **/ had been** born in a different era.
If only **you were / you'd been** paying attention in class instead of texting. You wouldn't have missed that explanation.
I do wish this meeting **didn't go / wasn't going** on quite so long. We all have other things to do!
If only Jamie **wasn't / hadn't been** injured. I'm certain the team would have won the match.
Imagine ………………… when the first steam engines were being built. How would your daily life have been different?
What if your neighbour's dog suddenly ………………… to you in perfect English right now? How would you react?
Would Edison have become so famous if he ………………… the lightbulb?
It's high time someone ………………… something about the broken heating in this building.
It's remarkable how Kate behaves as though she and her flatmates ………………… any tension between them at all.
………………… both of you like to take a seat while you wait?
If you ………………… to cancel your reservation, would you still be charged a fee?
Apparently, Hannah would rather we ………………… too much fuss about her leaving do next week.

sections.title: J: Key Word Transformation
sections.instructions: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given. Do not change the word given. You must use between three and eight words, including the word given.

items.sentence: Oliver doesn't want to be assigned the lead role in the presentation. rather Oliver a lead role in the presentation.
What a shame we didn't manage to get tickets for the concert. only If to get tickets for the concert!
Why don't the neighbours stop parking in front of our gate? wish I parking in front of our gate!
What a shame I didn't get to see her reaction when she won the award. could I wish her reaction when she won the award.
Don't you think the company should bring out an updated version of the software? about Don't you think it's out an updated version of the software?
I only found out about the cancellation because Miriam rang me. if I wouldn't have known about the cancellation me.
